title: Brain Abscess and Empyema
authors: Karen L. Roos, Kenneth L. Tyler

items.subtopic: Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding the Incidence and Pathogens of Brain Abscesses
items.content: A brain abscess is a focal, suppurative infection within the brain parenchyma. It has an incidence rate of approximately 0.3–1.3 per 100,000 persons annually. The pathogens vary based on immune status; in immunocompetent individuals, common bacteria include Streptococcus spp., Enterobacteriaceae, anaerobes, and staphylococci.
keyPoints: Incidence: ~0.3–1.3 per 100,000 persons/year
Common pathogens in immunocompetent individuals: Streptococcus spp. (40%), Enterobacteriaceae (25%), anaerobes (30%), staphylococci (10%)
In immunocompromised hosts: Nocardia spp., Toxoplasma gondii, Aspergillus spp., Candida spp., Cryptococcus neoformans
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.title: Etiology of Brain Abscesses: Routes of Infection
items.content: A brain abscess can develop through direct spread from contiguous infections, hematogenous spread, or following trauma/neurosurgery. Up to 25% are cryptogenic with no apparent primary source.
keyPoints: Routes of infection: Direct spread (otitis media, mastoiditis), hematogenous spread, post-trauma/surgery
Cryptogenic brain abscesses: up to 25%
Otitic abscesses common in temporal lobe (55–75%) and cerebellum (20–30%)
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.title: Pathogens Associated with Different Sources of Brain Abscesses
items.content: The pathogens causing brain abscesses vary depending on the source. Otitic abscesses often involve streptococci and anaerobes, while those from sinusitis may include Streptococcus milleri and Pseudomonas spp.
keyPoints: Otitic abscesses: streptococci, Bacteroides spp., Pseudomonas spp.
Sinusitis-related abscesses: Streptococcus milleri, Haemophilus spp., Staphylococcus aureus
Hematogenous abscesses often multiple and located in middle cerebral artery territory
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Stages of Brain Abscess Development and Imaging Correlation
items.content: Brain abscess formation progresses through distinct stages, each with specific histopathological features. Understanding these stages aids in correlating clinical presentation with imaging findings.
keyPoints: Bacterial invasion requires preexisting ischemia, necrosis, or hypoxemia.
Early cerebritis (days 1–3): Perivascular inflammatory cells and central coagulative necrosis.
Late cerebritis (days 4–9): Pus formation enlarges the necrotic center; edema becomes more distinct.
Early capsule formation (days 10–13): Capsule better developed on cortical side, correlates with ring-enhancing capsule on imaging.
Late capsule formation (day 14+): Well-formed necrotic center surrounded by dense collagenous capsule; marked gliosis develops.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · PATHOGENESIS AND HISTOPATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Symptomatology of Brain Abscess
items.content: Brain abscesses present variably, often as expanding intracranial masses. Key symptoms include headache, fever, seizures, and focal neurologic deficits.
keyPoints: Most patients present 11–12 days after symptom onset.
Headache occurs in >75% of patients; characterized by constant, dull, aching sensation.
Fever is present in only 50% at diagnosis; absence does not exclude the condition.
New onset seizures occur in 15–35% of patients.
Focal neurologic deficits are seen in >60% of patients.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · PATHOGENESIS AND HISTOPATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: MRI vs. CT in Brain Abscess Diagnosis
items.content: MRI is superior to CT for diagnosing brain abscesses, especially in early stages (cerebritis) and in the posterior fossa. MRI provides detailed imaging of cerebritis with specific signal characteristics on T1- and T2-weighted images.
keyPoints: MRI better than CT for early-stage abscesses and posterior fossa
Cerebritis: low signal on T1, irregular postgadolinium enhancement, increased signal on T2
CT shows cerebritis as hypodensity; mature abscess as focal hypodensity with ring enhancement
Glucocorticoids may alter CT/MRI appearance of the capsule
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Microbiologic Diagnosis and Laboratory Findings in Brain Abscess
items.content: Diagnosis of brain abscess etiology is best achieved through Gram's stain and culture from CT-guided stereotactic needle aspiration. Blood cultures are positive in ~10% of cases, but >85% for Listeria-related abscesses. Elevated inflammatory markers are common.
keyPoints: Gram's stain and culture via CT-guided needle aspiration
Aerobic/anaerobic, mycobacterial, fungal cultures recommended
Blood cultures positive in ~10% of cases; >85% for Listeria
Peripheral leukocytosis (50%), elevated ESR (60%), CRP (80%) common
Lumbar puncture contraindicated due to risk of herniation
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Key Differentiators for Brain Abscess Diagnosis
items.content: Brain abscess diagnosis is differentiated from other conditions like subdural empyema and meningitis by the presence of headache, fever, focal neurologic signs, and seizures. In absence of fever, brain tumors are considered.
keyPoints: Conditions causing similar symptoms include subdural empyema, bacterial meningitis, viral meningoencephalitis, superior sagittal sinus thrombosis, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis, primary/metastatic brain tumors, cerebral infarction, and hematoma.
Fever absence shifts differential to primary and metastatic brain tumors.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Brain Abscess | PROGNOSIS

items.title: Prognosis and Management of Brain Abscess
items.content: Brain abscess prognosis has improved with advancements in neuroimaging, surgical techniques, and antibiotics. Mortality is typically <15%, but ≥20% of survivors experience significant sequelae.
keyPoints: Mortality rate for brain abscess is typically <15%.
Sequelae such as seizures, weakness, aphasia, or mental impairment occur in ≥20% of survivors.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Brain Abscess | PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following terms is used to describe a nonencapsulated brain abscess?
items.explanation: The term 'cerebritis' is often employed to describe a nonencapsulated brain abscess.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.question: What is the approximate incidence of bacterial brain abscess per 100,000 persons per year?
items.explanation: The incidence of bacterial brain abscess is ~0.3–1.3 per 100,000 persons per year.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which pathogen is most commonly associated with brain abscesses in Latin America?
items.explanation: In Latin America, the most common cause of brain abscess is Taenia solium (neurocysticercosis).
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which type of brain abscesses are often multiple and show a predilection for the territory of the middle cerebral artery?
items.explanation: Hematogenous abscesses account for ~25% of brain abscesses and often show a predilection for the territory of the middle cerebral artery.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which stage of brain abscess formation is characterized by a well-developed capsule and regression of cerebral edema?
options: Late capsule formation stage (day 14 and beyond)
items.explanation: The late capsule formation stage is defined by a well-formed necrotic center surrounded by a dense collagenous capsule, with regression of cerebral edema.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · PATHOGENESIS AND HISTOPATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: What is the most common symptom in patients presenting with a brain abscess?
items.explanation: The most common symptom of a brain abscess is headache, occurring in more than 75% of patients.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · PATHOGENESIS AND HISTOPATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: What clinical triad is present in less than 50% of brain abscess cases?
options: Headache, fever, and focal neurologic deficit
Seizure activity, aphasia, and visual field defects
items.explanation: The classic clinical triad of headache, fever, and a focal neurologic deficit is present in less than 50% of brain abscess cases.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · PATHOGENESIS AND HISTOPATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: Which neuroimaging study is superior for demonstrating brain abscesses in the early cerebritis stage?
items.explanation: MRI is better than CT for demonstrating abscesses in the early cerebritis stages.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: On T2-weighted MRI, how does a mature brain abscess typically appear?
options: Hypointense central area with hyperintense capsule
Hyperintense central area of pus surrounded by hypointense capsule
No significant change from normal tissue
items.explanation: A mature brain abscess appears as a hyperintense central area of pus with a well-defined hypointense capsule on T2-weighted MRI.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic procedure should be avoided in patients with suspected focal intracranial infections like abscess or empyema?
items.explanation: Lumbar puncture should not be performed in patients with known or suspected focal intracranial infections due to the risk of herniation.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition is most likely to be considered as a differential diagnosis for brain abscess when fever is absent?
items.explanation: When fever is absent, primary and metastatic brain tumors become the major differential diagnosis.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Brain Abscess | PROGNOSIS

items.question: What is a key component of optimal therapy for brain abscesses?
options: High-dose parenteral antibiotics and neurosurgical drainage
items.explanation: Optimal therapy involves a combination of high-dose parenteral antibiotics and neurosurgical drainage.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Brain Abscess | PROGNOSIS

items.question: What is the typical mortality rate for brain abscess in modern series?
items.explanation: In modern series, the mortality rate is typically <15%.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Brain Abscess | PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.statement: Cryptogenic brain abscesses have an identifiable primary source of infection.
items.explanation: In up to 25% of cases, no obvious primary source of infection is apparent (cryptogenic brain abscess).
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.statement: Streptococcus spp. are the most common pathogens in hematogenous brain abscesses.
items.explanation: Streptococci are the most common pathogens in hematogenous brain abscesses, especially those associated with congenital cardiac malformations.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Meningismus is a common presentation in patients with a brain abscess.
items.explanation: Meningismus occurs only if the abscess has ruptured into the ventricle or the infection has spread to the subarachnoid space.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · PATHOGENESIS AND HISTOPATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: The early cerebritis stage of a brain abscess is characterized by pus formation and enlargement of the necrotic center.
items.explanation: Pus formation and enlargement of the necrotic center occur in the late cerebritis stage, not the early cerebritis stage.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · PATHOGENESIS AND HISTOPATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: Blood cultures are positive in approximately 85% of cases involving brain abscesses due to Listeria.
items.explanation: Blood cultures may be positive in >85% of patients with abscesses due to Listeria.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis is crucial for diagnosing brain abscess.
items.explanation: CSF analysis contributes nothing to the diagnosis or therapy of a brain abscess.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Medical therapy alone is optimal for all patients with brain abscess.
items.explanation: Medical therapy alone is not optimal and should be reserved for specific cases.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Brain Abscess | PROGNOSIS

items.statement: Prophylactic anticonvulsant therapy is recommended due to the high risk of seizures in brain abscess patients.
items.explanation: Patients should receive prophylactic anticonvulsant therapy because of the high risk (~35%) of focal or generalized seizures.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Brain Abscess | PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.assertion: Otitogenic brain abscesses predominantly occur in the temporal lobe.
items.reason: Otitogenic abscesses are associated with cholesteatomas, which affect the temporal bone.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why otogenic abscesses predominantly occur in the temporal lobe.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.assertion: Paranasal sinusitis is associated with brain abscesses primarily located in the frontal lobes.
items.reason: Abscesses from paranasal sinusitis often spread directly to adjacent frontal lobes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why abscesses associated with paranasal sinusitis are primarily located in the frontal lobes.
items.reference: Brain Abscess and Empyema · Brain Abscess and Empyema | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
